summary: DHH expresses frustration with people who bring up topics in conversation without contributing their own thoughts or analysis. He draws a distinction between online lurking (harmless) and offline freeriding (annoying), arguing that people who recite something they read without wrapping it in their own perspective are essentially stealing others' thinking. The post uses the scenario of three people in a room where one person raises a topic but contributes nothing, leaving the other two to do all the intellectual work. He calls this 'thought theft' and demands that people at least offer something of their own when they introduce a discussion topic.
key_points: There's a meaningful difference between freeriding on discussions online (harmless) versus offline (problematic)
Bringing up a topic by merely reciting a source without adding your own perspective is a form of intellectual leeching
People who do this are essentially outsourcing their thinking to others, then storing those opinions for reuse elsewhere
The minimum bar for participating in a discussion is contributing something — not necessarily clever or original, just something
money_quotes: What I don't love is people freeriding on interesting discussions when they can't be bothered to give the subject matter enough runs on the internal track to come up with something to add.
Expecting others to do their thinking for them, so maybe they can store it for reuse with different people. Thought theft, I'm telling you.

One guy brings up a topic, but only by reciting the source. That's it. Then it's up to the other two guys to offer their slice of commentary, kicking the ball around, while the third guy just consumes the opinions.
I'm not fantasising about always getting clever and original thoughts thrown at me. Just give me something, dammit. Leeching thoughts is lame.
key_insight: If you're going to introduce a topic into a conversation, you owe the group at least your own take on it — anything less is intellectual freeloading.
